text: The surgeon begins the peritoneal dissection on the left side, measuring approximately eight centimeters above the internal ring to determine the starting point.
The surgeon inspects the area where the hernia was located, observing the reduced sigmoid colon that was previously in the hernia.
The surgeon observes a medial ligament herniating into the defect, inspecting the anatomical structures involved in the hernia.
The surgeon creates a lateral flap, ensuring the transversalis fascia remains on the superior aspect of the dissection field.
The surgeon transitions to the pre-transversalis space to gain access to the parietal compartment, preparing to take down the bladder.
The surgeon develops the plane in the pre-transversalis space, keeping the transversalis fascia elevated. The dissection progresses medially before moving laterally.
The surgeon performs a preliminary dissection in zone one, located lateral to the epigastric vessels, preparing the area to accommodate an extra-large mid-weight Bard mesh.
The surgeon transitions to zone two, entering the pre-transversalis space into the parietal compartment, where the rectus muscle becomes visible.
The surgeon begins to take down the bladder, focusing on the pre-transversalis space to access the parietal compartment.
The surgeon extends the medial flap by cauterizing the medial umbilical ligament to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon continues the zone two dissection, carefully removing adipose tissue from the surface of the pubic bone, which is visible in the surgical field.
During the dissection, the surgeon identifies a direct inguinal hernia with a pseudosac and proceeds to reduce the hernia by carefully removing the pseudosac.
The surgeon uses a fourth arm to assist in retracting tissue, facilitating the dissection process.
An eight millimeter port is positioned laterally on the patient's left side, assisting in retracting tissues during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the direct inguinal hernia, carefully separating the hernia sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon transitions to the indirect space, addressing the sliding component of the hernia visible in this area.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to retract tissue towards the patient's head while dissecting the space around the inguinal hernia.
The surgeon dissects the indirect space, revealing the hernia sac medially. Retraction is applied while anteriorly buzzing and pushing the tissue to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon identifies a sizable cord lipoma associated with the hernia sac and uses the fourth arm to retract the tissue towards the patient's head, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon searches for the cord structures and begins to dissect the hernia sac away from these structures, using careful manipulation to separate them.
The surgeon carefully follows the tinting of the peritoneum while attempting to retract the sliding hernia, which presents challenges in achieving optimal retraction.
The surgeon identifies the end of the peritoneum and prepares to take it down, with the structure clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the area where the peritoneum needs to be dissected down, located under the epigastric vessels. The median umbilical ligament is seen herniating into the direct space, and the surgeon carefully removes it.
The surgeon returns to the zone two dissection, ensuring completion two centimeters below the pubis. An obturator hernia plug is visible, which will be addressed later.

The surgeon navigates through a scarred area, carefully dissecting to find the correct plane without creating defects in the peritoneum. The dissection proceeds laterally, maintaining proximity to the peritoneum to avoid nerve injury.

The surgeon identifies a cord lipoma or retroperitoneal fat herniating into the indirect space alongside the cord structures, carefully delineating the boundary between the cord structures and the retroperitoneal fat.
The surgeon identifies a plane within the surgical field and begins transecting tissue, removing additional tissue as it is discovered.
The surgeon identifies a large defect in the indirect space, visible as a track, and proceeds to address the obturator hernia plug.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to provide elevated retraction, enhancing the view for continued dissection. Care is taken to avoid damaging the vessels associated with the spermatic cord.
The surgeon hesitates while clearly visualizing the spermatic cord within the surgical field.
The obturator artery is visible, and the surgeon encounters bleeding while dissecting near the spermatic cord vessels. A Raytech sponge is introduced to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon addresses bleeding by starting on the right side, where a large direct hernia is visible. A flap is created approximately eight centimeters above the internal ring, with efforts to keep the transversalis fascia elevated, particularly laterally, before transitioning to the pre-transversalis fascia medially.

The surgeon leaves a portion of the peritoneum in the midline while performing bilateral dissection. The rectus muscle is visible in the parietal compartment, with the epigastric vessels centered on the screen. The transversalis fascia is being maintained in an elevated position laterally.

The surgeon continues the flap dissection, staying close to the rectus muscle, and connects the space from the previously dissected left side. Fat is brought down to clear the pubic symphysis completely, while improving retraction to achieve hemostasis.

The surgeon removes nearly all the fat from the anterior abdominal wall to accommodate two meshes. A large direct defect on the right side is visible, easily descending from the direct space. Additionally, a femoral hernia component is observed.

The surgeon applies Ratex liberally to maintain hemostasis while dissecting underneath the epigastric vessels. The dissection continues laterally, aiming to keep the surgical field clear and manageable in the confined space.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the peritoneum, searching for an indirect hernia. A small amount of fat is visible in the femoral space, which the surgeon assesses to ensure there is no significant obstruction. The iliac vein is visible medially, confirming the absence of a large fat plug in the hernia.
The surgeon examines the indirect space, identifying a small indirect defect. The hernia sac is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon inspects the right side for a core lipoma, observing the allopubic tract without finding a significant lipoma. The peritoneum is dissected down to ensure the mesh fits smoothly without crinkling or clamshelling.

The surgeon positions the spermatic cord medially and continues to dissect the peritoneum to achieve the desired triangular configuration. The dissection is checked in the femoral space, located two centimeters below the pubis, with additional dissection needed to properly splay the spermatic cord and associated vessels.

The surgeon achieves the desired triangular configuration in the surgical field and returns to the left side to address previous bleeding, performing additional dissection to further expose the cord lipoma.
The surgeon reviews the surgical field, observing a large lateral vessel that appears to be supplying the cord lipoma. The vessel is left intact due to its size.
The surgeon inspects the left side, ensuring the cord lipoma is adequately mobilized and performs additional dissection beneath the pubis and within the obturator space.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum to check for movement, confirming satisfaction with the dissection. Stitches are prepared for securing the area.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to secure the mesh in place and a 3-0 V-Loc suture, approximately nine inches long, to close the peritoneum.
The surgeon places a stitch on the right side in preparation for further use, ensuring it remains secure while working on bilateral sides.

The surgeon positions the left and right extra-large mid-weight meshes, aligning the transverse line near the inguinal ligament and the vertical line close to the vas or between pelvic triangles. The meshes fit well without clamshelling, and the surgeon ensures they are tucked below the bladder to prevent clamshelling during bladder distension.
The surgeon sutures the mesh to Cooper's ligament, placing stitches in the anterior lateral and anterior medial positions to ensure stability and prevent movement.
The surgeon carefully places a stitch anterior medially, avoiding the inferior epigastric vessels. A single stitch is then placed through both meshes and Cooper's ligament to secure them in place.

The surgeon passes off the stitch and proceeds to close the peritoneal defects, leaving the median umbilical ligament intact. The insufflation pressure is reduced to about ten for the closure process.
The surgeon performs a canal stitch, ensuring no exposed barbs from the V-lock to prevent small bowel obstruction. The fourth arm is used to hold up the peritoneum for efficient sewing.

The surgeon runs the suture back a few throws and then moves to the other side using another 9-inch 3-0 suture. The lateral area appears thin, and the surgeon adjusts the retraction with the fourth arm to improve laxity, ensuring the tissue is redundant enough to cover the defect.
The surgeon identifies a hole in the peritoneum and prepares to close it using a vicryl stitch, employing a figure-eight technique if possible.

The surgeon uses a vicryl suture to place a figure-eight stitch in the peritoneum. All stitches and ray tech are removed, and the surgeon gently pushes up to decompress and ensure the mesh is positioned correctly.
